nodes.title: 10x Factor
nodes.blurb: The 10x Factor is the value-differential threshold Pereira identifies as the entry ticket to exponential growth.

nodes.title: Affiliate Marketing
nodes.blurb: Affiliate marketing is a business model where third-party publishers promote another company's products and earn a commission on resulting sales or actions.

nodes.blurb: Churn rate is the percentage of customers or revenue lost during a specific time period.

nodes.title: Cost Leadership
nodes.blurb: Cost leadership is the strategy of offering products or services at a lower cost than competitors while maintaining acceptable quality.

nodes.title: Critical Mass
nodes.blurb: Critical mass is the threshold level of network participation at which the value of a product or service to each user exceeds its cost, triggering self-sustaining growth.

nodes.title: Crowdfunding Models
nodes.blurb: Crowdfunding raises capital for a venture from a large number of contributors via online platforms.

nodes.title: Customer Acquisition Cost
nodes.blurb: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) is the total cost of marketing and sales efforts required to acquire a single new customer.

nodes.title: Customer Conversion Rate
nodes.blurb: Customer conversion rate is the percentage of interested prospects or website visitors who complete a desired action — most commonly becoming a paying customer.

nodes.title: Customer Engagement Score
nodes.blurb: Customer Engagement Score (CES) is a quantitative measure of how actively users interact with a product or service.

nodes.title: Customer Lifetime Value
nodes.blurb: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV or LTV) is the total revenue a business can reasonably expect from a single customer account over the entire duration of the relationship.

nodes.title: Customer Personas
nodes.blurb: Customer personas are fictional but research-grounded representations of a firm's ideal customers, built from demographic, psychographic, and behavioral data.

nodes.title: Customer Relationships
nodes.blurb: Customer Relationships is the BMC block that defines the type of interaction a firm establishes with each customer segment — across acquisition, retention, and sales expansion.

nodes.blurb: Customer Segmentation Models are the seven approaches for dividing a market into addressable groups, each emphasizing different attributes of the customer.

nodes.title: Customer Segments
nodes.blurb: Customer Segments is the BMC block that defines the distinct groups of people or organizations a business aims to serve.

nodes.title: Data Network Effect
nodes.blurb: A data network effect occurs when a product becomes more valuable as more users use it because each interaction generates data that improves the product itself for all users.

nodes.title: Direct Network Effect
nodes.blurb: A direct (or same-side) network effect occurs when each additional user of a product directly raises the value of that product for all other users of the same type.

nodes.title: Direct vs Indirect Distribution
nodes.blurb: Distribution channels are the pathways through which a product moves from producer to customer.

nodes.title: Direct vs Indirect Network Effects
nodes.blurb: Network effects describe how a product or service becomes more valuable as more people use it.

nodes.blurb: The Direct-to-Consumer (D2C) model has the manufacturer or brand sell directly to end customers, bypassing wholesalers, distributors, and retailers.

nodes.blurb: Distribution channels are the pathways through which products and services reach end customers.

nodes.title: Distribution Partnership
nodes.blurb: A distribution partnership enables companies to extend their market reach by sharing distribution channels, networks, or routes-to-market with another organization.

nodes.title: Doom Loop
nodes.blurb: The Doom Loop is the Flywheel Effect's mirror image: a self-reinforcing negative cycle in which momentum decays rather than compounds.

nodes.title: Ecosystem-Based Business Model
nodes.blurb: An ecosystem-based business model is a portfolio of products, services, or companies that interoperate to deliver a coordinated end-user experience and produce compounding lock-in.

nodes.title: Employee Empowerment
nodes.blurb: Employee empowerment is the deliberate practice of giving employees the authority, autonomy, and trust to make decisions, take risks, and own outcomes within their domains.

nodes.blurb: Growth and scaling are commonly confused but structurally different.

nodes.blurb: A local network effect is one whose value accrues only to users within a bounded geography, cluster, or interest community — not to the global network.

nodes.blurb: A loyalty program rewards repeat customers with points, discounts, exclusive access, or status tiers to incentivize ongoing patronage.

nodes.blurb: A marketplace is a platform that connects buyers and sellers of goods or services — facilitating transactions but not taking inventory.

nodes.blurb: The foundational distinction in AI capability.

nodes.blurb: Pricing psychology is the application of cognitive and behavioral biases to how prices are set, displayed, and perceived.

nodes.title: Product Development Lifecycle (9 Stages)
nodes.blurb: Pereira frames new product development as a 9-stage progression from idea to sustained growth, each stage demanding distinct skills, deliverables, and decision criteria.

nodes.title: Product Launch Playbook
nodes.blurb: The Product Launch Playbook is Pereira's three-phase framework for executing a launch as a sustained motion, not a single-day event.

nodes.title: Product-Led Growth

nodes.title: Product-Market Fit
nodes.blurb: Product-market fit (PMF) means your product addresses the real problems of a real target market — at sufficient scale to support growth and profitability.

nodes.title: Reed's Law

nodes.title: SaaS Business Model

nodes.title: SaaS Company Stages
nodes.blurb: The four-stage lifecycle of a SaaS company: Pre-startup, Startup, Growth, and Maturity.

nodes.title: SaaS Metrics Suite
nodes.blurb: The core metrics that measure the health, growth, and sustainability of a SaaS business.

nodes.title: SaaS Monetization Strategies

nodes.title: SaaS Pricing Models
nodes.blurb: The five primary pricing architectures for SaaS businesses: Freemium, Flat-Rate, Tiered, Per-User, and Usage-Based.

nodes.title: SaaS Renewal Pricing

nodes.title: SaaS Rule of 40
nodes.blurb: The SaaS Rule of 40 is a business heuristic that asserts a healthy SaaS company's growth rate plus profit margin should sum to at least 40%.

nodes.title: Sales Enablement
nodes.blurb: Sales Enablement is the discipline of equipping sales teams with the knowledge, content, tools, training, and coaching they need to engage buyers effectively and close deals.

nodes.title: Sales-Led GTM
nodes.blurb: A Sales-Led Go-to-Market strategy makes the sales team the primary engine of customer acquisition.

nodes.title: Sales-Led vs Product-Led GTM
nodes.blurb: A Go-to-Market (GTM) methodology is the core mechanism by which a company acquires and converts customers.

nodes.title: Scalability

nodes.title: Scalable Business Model
nodes.blurb: A scalable business model is one that can increase output — users, revenue, market reach — without a proportional increase in resources, costs, or operational complexity.

nodes.title: Servitization
nodes.blurb: Servitization is the strategic shift from selling products outright to selling services or outcomes around those products.

nodes.title: Single-Feature MVP
nodes.blurb: A Single-Feature MVP is a high-fidelity MVP that contains only the core feature of your future product — nothing else.

nodes.title: Six Business Model Shifts
nodes.blurb: A taxonomy of six structural shifts modern business models are undergoing — patterns of evolution that successful firms are riding while laggards risk obsolescence.

nodes.title: Six Types of Customer Relationships
nodes.blurb: A framework cataloguing the six structural modes a company can use to relate to its customers, drawn from the Business Model Canvas tradition and elaborated by Pereira.

nodes.blurb: Strategic Group Analysis identifies clusters of firms within an industry that share similar competitive strategies — cost structure, product range, distribution

nodes.title: Strategic Partnership Types
nodes.blurb: Strategic partnerships take distinct structural forms depending on objectives, risk tolerance, resource commitment, and desired level of integration.

nodes.title: Strategic Partnerships
nodes.blurb: Strategic partnerships are formal alliances between two or more organizations to achieve mutual business objectives that would be difficult or impossible to achieve independently.

nodes.title: Subscription Box Model
nodes.blurb: The Subscription Box model delivers a curated or replenishment shipment of physical goods to subscribers on a recurring schedule (typically monthly).

nodes.title: Subscription Business Model
nodes.blurb: A subscription business model is a revenue model in which customers pay a recurring fee — weekly, monthly, or yearly — to access a product or service.

nodes.title: Subscription Fatigue
nodes.blurb: Subscription fatigue is the state of consumer exhaustion caused by an oversupply of subscription offerings across products and services.

nodes.title: Subscription Maturity Model
nodes.blurb: The Subscription Maturity Model is a five-stage framework describing the operational evolution of a subscription-based business.

nodes.title: Subscription Revenue Cycle
nodes.blurb: The Subscription Revenue Cycle is a five-step operating framework describing how subscription businesses generate and compound revenue over time.

nodes.title: Switching Costs
nodes.blurb: Switching costs are the total cost — time, money, effort, risk — a customer incurs when moving from one provider's product to another's.

nodes.title: SWOT Analysis
nodes.blurb: SWOT analysis is a structured strategic diagnosis tool that maps a firm across four quadrants: Strengths (internal advantages), Weaknesses (internal limitations

nodes.title: Take-Rate Economics
nodes.blurb: Take-rate economics describes how platforms, marketplaces, and brokerages capture value: by taking a percentage of each transaction they facilitate.

nodes.title: TAM SAM SOM Sizing
nodes.blurb: TAM/SAM/SOM is a three-layer market sizing tactic used to demonstrate that a market is large enough to support the financial investment required to pursue it.

nodes.title: Technology Partnership
nodes.blurb: A technology partnership is a strategic collaboration centered on the exchange of technological knowledge, intellectual property, or innovations between organizations.

nodes.title: Transaction-Based API Model
nodes.blurb: The transaction-based API model charges developers based on the number of transactions or discrete interactions made through the API.

nodes.title: Triple Bottom Line
nodes.blurb: The Triple Bottom Line expands the definition of value creation beyond financial performance to include social impact (people) and environmental impact (planet) alongside profit.

nodes.title: Two-Sided Network Effects

nodes.title: Unique Selling Proposition
nodes.blurb: A Unique Selling Proposition (USP) is a concise, strategic statement that captures why a prospective customer should choose this product over alternatives.

nodes.title: Value Creation
nodes.blurb: Value creation is the process of turning labor and resources into something that meets the needs of others — tangible goods, services, experiences, or ideas.

nodes.title: Value Drivers
nodes.blurb: Value drivers are the specific features, attributes, or benefits that create value for customers — the underlying levers that determine whether they choose, ret

nodes.title: Value Proposition
nodes.blurb: A value proposition is the specific, unique benefit a firm offers customers that differentiates it from competitors — articulated from the customer's point of v

nodes.title: Value Proposition and USP
nodes.blurb: A Unique Selling Proposition (USP) is the concise articulation of what makes a product or service distinct from and superior to alternatives.

nodes.title: Vanity Metrics vs Actionable Metrics
nodes.blurb: The distinction between vanity metrics and actionable metrics is one of the bedrock concepts of Lean Startup analytics.

nodes.title: Viral Growth Loops
nodes.blurb: A viral growth loop is a self-reinforcing cycle in which every new user contributes to acquiring more users, creating compounding growth.

nodes.title: Virtuous Cycle

nodes.title: White-Labeling Model
nodes.blurb: White-labeling is a revenue model where a producer manufactures or develops a product/service that another company then rebrands and sells as its own.
